title: Losing the Narrative
subtitle: Communication Tempo, Expectation Asymmetry, and Perception Effects in the First Week of the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran War
title_normalized: losing the narrative: communication tempo, expectation asymmetry, and perception effects in the first week of the 2026 u.s.-israel-iran war

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Wu (2026), Losing the Narrative, EPINOVA Working Paper F–2026–04
alternate_identifiers.description: Short citation shown or implied by the EPINOVA working paper format

description: This working paper examines communication tempo, expectation asymmetry, and perception effects during the first week of the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran War. Using a best-effort cross-channel estimate of visible official communication outputs between February 28 and March 6, 2026, it compares the United States, Israel, and Iran and argues that perceived narrative momentum was shaped by disclosure rhythm, narrative continuity, media-format compatibility, and expectation asymmetry rather than kinetic developments alone.
abstract: This working paper examines why the United States appeared to underperform in the information environment during the first week of the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran war, even though battlefield outcomes remained contested. The paper argues that perceptions of narrative momentum were shaped less by kinetic developments alone than by structural differences in official disclosure tempo, narrative continuity, media-format compatibility, and expectation asymmetry across the principal actors. To explore these dynamics, the paper employs a best-effort cross-channel estimate of visible official communication outputs published between February 28, 2026, 00:00 and March 6, 2026, 22:00. Under this estimate, Iran generated approximately 342 official or officially attributed outputs, the United States approximately 200, and Israel approximately 165, for a combined total of roughly 707 items. These figures are used analytically as indicators of visible communication tempo rather than as a fully audited URL-level census. The analysis suggests that differences in communication rhythm and narrative architecture significantly shaped perception effects during the conflict’s first week. Actors whose messaging appeared more continuous, serially structured, and adapted to feed-based media environments were better positioned to influence how audiences interpreted the trajectory of the conflict. In conflicts characterized by strong asymmetries of capability, expectation asymmetry can further amplify these dynamics, as losses by the materially stronger side often receive disproportionate attention in the information environment. The United States did not necessarily lose militarily in the war’s first week. However, its official communication appeared less continuous, less serially structured, and less adapted to high-tempo digital information environments than that of Iran and Israel, contributing to the perception that it had lost narrative initiative during the early phase of the conflict.

series_information.series_description: The WP-F series explores AI governance and strategic risk through comparative and historical diagnostics, examining how inherited strategic and regulatory logics break down when transferred into AI-mediated contexts.

methodology.framework_name: Communication Tempo and Expectation Asymmetry Framework
methodology.analytical_scope: Best-effort cross-channel estimate of visible official communication outputs during the first week of the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran War.

observation_window.description: Observation window used in the PDF to approximate the first week of the war.

methodology.method_type: Qualitative and descriptive cross-channel communication-tempo assessment; not a fully audited archival census.
principal_channels: Iranian ministry-linked and state-affiliated communication channels

core_variables.name: Official disclosure tempo
core_variables.description: Frequency and rhythm of visible official communication outputs during the observation window.

core_variables.name: Narrative continuity
core_variables.description: Degree to which communications appear as an ongoing, cumulative stream rather than disconnected releases.

core_variables.name: Media-format compatibility
core_variables.description: Fit between official communication format and feed-based digital information environments.

core_variables.name: Expectation asymmetry
core_variables.description: Perception effect in which setbacks by the materially stronger side receive disproportionate attention relative to losses by weaker actors.

estimated_outputs.primary_channels: Ministry statements, IRGC-linked communiqués, IRNA releases, and other state-affiliated channels
estimated_outputs.note: Serial retaliation framing likely increased visible communication tempo.

estimated_outputs.primary_channels: White House publications, Department of Defense releases, official remarks, and press briefings
Israel Defense Forces operational pages and government updates
estimated_outputs.note: Strong continuity through rolling timeline-style updates.

estimated_outputs.primary_channels: All principal official channels included in the estimate
estimated_outputs.note: Used as an indicator of relative communication tempo, not as a fully audited documentary total.

communication_ratio.description: Approximate relative visible official communication tempo reported in the conclusion.

limitations: The output estimate is a best-effort cross-channel estimate, not a fully audited URL-level census.
Official communication architectures differ across actors, including rolling live-update pages and discrete standalone releases.
The analysis focuses on tempo and structure rather than the factual accuracy, credibility, or substantive quality of individual messages.

dimensions.name: Communication Tempo
dimensions.description: Visible frequency and rhythm of official outputs across actor communication channels.

dimensions.name: Narrative Continuity
dimensions.description: Continuity of messaging streams and ability to maintain perceived real-time presence in the information environment.

dimensions.name: Seriality
dimensions.description: Use of sequential, wave-based, or numbered communication structures that create an interpretable storyline of progression.

dimensions.name: Media-Format Compatibility
dimensions.description: Compatibility of official communication outputs with feed-based and high-velocity digital media environments.

dimensions.name: Expectation Asymmetry
dimensions.description: Asymmetric interpretation of battlefield events, where losses by stronger actors are amplified as evidence of underperformance.

dimensions.name: Narrative Momentum
dimensions.description: Perceived initiative generated through cumulative communication tempo, continuity, serial framing, and expectation management.

key_findings: The United States did not necessarily lose militarily during the first week of the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran War, but it appeared to lose narrative initiative in the information environment.
Perceptions of narrative momentum were shaped less by kinetic outcomes alone than by official disclosure tempo, narrative continuity, media-format compatibility, and expectation asymmetry.

A best-effort cross-channel estimate identifies approximately 707 visible official communication outputs during February 28–March 6, 2026: Iran approximately 342, the United States approximately 200, and Israel approximately 165.
Iran’s visible communication tempo accounted for nearly half of the estimated official outputs and benefited from serial retaliation framing.
U.S. communication was dispersed across multiple institutions, generating substantial volume but weakening the appearance of a single dominant narrative structure.
Israel’s rolling timeline-style operational updates created strong continuity and real-time visibility in feed-based information environments.

Expectation asymmetry amplified perception effects because localized damage or successful retaliation by the weaker actor could be interpreted as evidence that the stronger side was failing to achieve expected dominance.
Wartime communication effectiveness depends not only on message content or battlefield outcomes, but also on tempo, continuity, structural design, and expectation dynamics.

disclaimer: This metadata record is generated from the uploaded PDF and early ORCID-derived metadata. The working paper presents diagnostic and exploratory analysis intended to examine inherited strategic and governance logics under AI-mediated conditions. It does not constitute policy recommendations, predictive assessments, or official positions of any institution.
